questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Notion Academy as described in the article?

options.text: To sell Notion templates in a marketplace
options.explanation: Selling templates is a feature of a lesson, not the overall purpose of the Academy.

options.text: To provide curated learning paths, badges, and certifications for Notion users
options.explanation: The article states that Notion Academy offers learning paths, badges, and certifications to help users build capabilities.

options.text: To host live webinars exclusively for enterprise customers
options.explanation: Webinars are mentioned, but they are part of broader resources, not the sole purpose.

options.text: To replace the Notion Help Center as the main support hub
options.explanation: The Help Center remains a separate resource for detailed questions.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the "Notion Certified Admin" badge?

options.text: It is awarded after completing a single 10‑minute lesson on workspace design.
options.explanation: Certification requires more comprehensive learning than a single short lesson.

options.text: It recognizes the ability to run Notion at scale, implement security best practices, and is available for any plan type.
options.explanation: The article explicitly mentions these criteria for the Certified Admin badge.

options.text: It is only available to users on Notion’s Enterprise plan.
options.explanation: The badge is offered for any plan type, not limited to Enterprise.

options.text: It is granted automatically when a user creates a template for the marketplace.
options.explanation: Creating a template does not automatically confer the admin certification.

questions.question: If a beginner wants to learn how to navigate Notion, create blocks, and build basic databases, which path should they enroll in?

options.text: Advanced Path
options.explanation: The Advanced Path focuses on automation and complex capabilities, not basics.

options.text: Essentials Path
options.explanation: The Essentials Path is described as the starting point for new users to gain basic skills.

options.text: Workflows Path
options.explanation: Workflows Path is intermediate and focuses on connected systems.

options.text: Templates & Marketplace Lesson
options.explanation: That lesson is about creating and selling templates, not foundational navigation.

questions.question: Which lesson would most directly help a team reduce repetitive manual tasks by using scheduled or trigger‑based automation?

options.text: Custom Agents
options.explanation: The Custom Agents lesson explains how to automate repeat work using schedules or triggers.

options.text: Sharing & Collaboration
options.explanation: That lesson focuses on teamwork, not automation.

options.text: Project Management
options.explanation: Project Management teaches database setup, not automation of busywork.

options.text: Workspace Design
options.explanation: Workspace Design covers best practices for layout, not automation.

questions.question: How does the article suggest users can validate the skills they acquire through Notion Academy?

options.text: By completing a final exam that is administered by Notion’s support team.
options.explanation: There is no mention of a support‑team administered exam.

options.text: By earning badges and, for certain paths, achieving Notion certifications.
options.explanation: The article highlights badges and the Notion Certified Admin credential as validation mechanisms.

options.text: By publishing at least three templates in the Notion Marketplace.
options.explanation: Publishing templates is optional and not tied to skill validation.

options.text: By attending a live webinar and receiving a participation certificate.
options.explanation: Webinars are resources, but the article does not link them to formal validation.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the difference between a "Lesson" and a "Path" in Notion Academy?

options.text: A Lesson is a single, self‑contained topic; a Path is a curated series of Lessons that together build a broader competency.
options.explanation: The article lists individual lessons and groups them into Paths, indicating this hierarchical relationship.

options.text: A Lesson is only available to paid subscribers, while a Path is free for all users.
options.explanation: Access tiers are not described in the article.

options.text: A Lesson always lasts less than 10 minutes, whereas a Path takes several hours to complete.
options.explanation: Lesson durations vary; the distinction is about content grouping, not length.

options.text: A Lesson focuses on technical skills, while a Path focuses exclusively on soft‑skill development.
options.explanation: Both Lessons and Paths cover a mix of technical and collaborative topics.

questions.question: What resource does the article direct users to for “quick answers to your most detailed questions”?

options.text: Notion Academy’s live webinar schedule
options.explanation: Webinars are for skill development, not quick Q&A.

options.text: The Notion Help Center
options.explanation: The article explicitly mentions the Help Center for quick answers.

options.text: The Notion Marketplace
options.explanation: Marketplace is for templates, not Q&A.

options.text: Custom Agents documentation
options.explanation: Custom Agents are a lesson topic, not a Q&A hub.

questions.question: Which path is described as focusing on “automating work, learning workspace architecture best practices, and harnessing Notion’s more complex capabilities”?

options.text: Essentials Path
options.explanation: Essentials Path is for beginners and basic skills.

options.text: Workflows Path
options.explanation: Workflows Path emphasizes connected systems and AI, not the advanced automation focus described.

options.text: Advanced Path
options.explanation: The article defines the Advanced Path with exactly those goals.

options.text: Templates & Marketplace Path
options.explanation: That is a single lesson, not a path, and focuses on template creation.
